content.problemStatement:
You are given a tree with NNN vertices (numbered 111 through NNN) and a bag with NNN markers. There is an integer written on each marker; each of these integers is 000, 111 or 222. You must assign exactly one marker to each vertex.

Let's define the unattractiveness of the resulting tree as the maximum absolute difference of integers written on the markers in any two vertices which are connected by an edge.

Find the minimum possible unattractiveness of the resulting tree.
constraints: The graph described on the input is a tree
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains a single integer N. The second line contains N space-separated integers denoting the numbers on markers in the bag. Each of the next N-1 lines contains two space-separated integers u and v denoting an edge between vertices u and v.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the minimum unattractiveness.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Example case 1: The minimum unattractiveness is 1 because we can assign the markers 0, 1, and 1 to the vertices 1, 2, and 3 respectively. Example case 2: The minimum unattractiveness is 1 because we can assign the markers 0, 1, and 2 to the vertices 1, 2, and 3 respectively. Example case 3: The minimum unattractiveness is 0 because we can assign the markers 2, 2, 2, and 2 to the vertices 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a greedy approach. We first sort the markers in ascending order. Then, we assign the smallest marker to the first vertex, the second smallest marker to the second vertex, and so on. We continue this process until all vertices have been assigned a marker. The unattractiveness of the resulting tree is the maximum absolute difference of integers written on the markers in any two vertices which are connected by an edge.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N log N) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the number of vertices. The sorting operation takes O(N log N) time, and we perform this operation for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — where N is the number of vertices. We need to store the markers and the edges of the tree.

code.Python3:
import sys

T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    N = int(input())
    markers = list(map(int, input().split()))
    edges = [list(map(int, input().split())) for _ in range(N - 1)]
    markers.sort()
    min_unattractiveness = 0
    for u, v in edges:
        if abs(markers[u - 1] - markers[v - 1]) > min_unattractiveness:
            min_unattractiveness = abs(markers[u - 1] - markers[v - 1])
    print(min_unattractiveness)
